CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and the main contributions are stated early and repeatedly (contextual LSTMs and hierarchical fusion). Writing quality is reported as good and related work / hyperparameters are adequately covered, and figures/tables appear informative for the experimental claims. From a clarity perspective I would have liked tighter, explicitly defined notation around the two-level fusion and a couple more schematic examples to make the hierarchical mechanism unambiguous, but these are minor presentation issues rather than fundamental obstacles to understanding. | B(4): The paper is well-organized with a standard logical flow (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and clearly states its main contributions early (contextual LSTM variants and hierarchical fusion). Notation and experimental details (including hyperparameters) are reported, and overall writing quality is good, aiding reproducibility. Figures/tables and examples appear adequate but not highlighted as exceptionally informative in the summary, so there may be minor presentation polish possible. From a clarity/presentation perspective it reads well with only small room for improvement.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and the main contributions are stated early and repeatedly (contextual LSTMs and hierarchical fusion). Writing quality is reported as good and related work / hyperparameters are adequately covered, and figures/tables appear informative for the experimental claims. From a clarity perspective I would have liked tighter, explicitly defined notation around the two-level fusion and a couple more schematic examples to make the hierarchical mechanism unambiguous, but these are minor presentation issues rather than fundamental obstacles to understanding.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well-organized with a standard logical flow (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and clearly states its main contributions early (contextual LSTM variants and hierarchical fusion). Notation and experimental details (including hyperparameters) are reported, and overall writing quality is good, aiding reproducibility. Figures/tables and examples appear adequate but not highlighted as exceptionally informative in the summary, so there may be minor presentation polish possible. From a clarity/presentation perspective it reads well with only small room for improvement.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares to reasonable baselines (uni-SVM) and prior multimodal methods including Poria et al. (2015) and Rozgic et al. (2012b) on standard datasets (MOSI, MOUD, IEMOCAP) using the same metrics, which supports fair empirical comparison. However, from the summary there is no evidence of comparison to more recent deep multimodal fusion alternatives or thorough component-level ablations and statistical significance testing; code is not mentioned either. The authors report gains over prior SOTA but do not appear to highlight cases where prior methods match or exceed performance, and reproducibility artifacts are limited—hence a minor but important omission in baseline breadth and analysis. | B(4): The paper evaluates on standard multimodal benchmarks (MOSI, MOUD, IEMOCAP) and compares to unimodal and prior multimodal methods (Poria et al. 2015 MKL; Rozgic et al. 2012b) using the same datasets and metrics, which supports fair empirical comparison. However, only a few prior multimodal baselines are included (no mention of more recent deep learning fusion approaches), and the summary does not indicate explicit acknowledgment when prior methods perform comparably, so the comparison is strong but not fully comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares to reasonable baselines (uni-SVM) and prior multimodal methods including Poria et al. (2015) and Rozgic et al. (2012b) on standard datasets (MOSI, MOUD, IEMOCAP) using the same metrics, which supports fair empirical comparison. However, from the summary there is no evidence of comparison to more recent deep multimodal fusion alternatives or thorough component-level ablations and statistical significance testing; code is not mentioned either. The authors report gains over prior SOTA but do not appear to highlight cases where prior methods match or exceed performance, and reproducibility artifacts are limited—hence a minor but important omission in baseline breadth and analysis.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates on standard multimodal benchmarks (MOSI, MOUD, IEMOCAP) and compares to unimodal and prior multimodal methods (Poria et al. 2015 MKL; Rozgic et al. 2012b) using the same datasets and metrics, which supports fair empirical comparison. However, only a few prior multimodal baselines are included (no mention of more recent deep learning fusion approaches), and the summary does not indicate explicit acknowledgment when prior methods perform comparably, so the comparison is strong but not fully comprehensive.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper states clear claims and shows consistent empirical gains on standard datasets, but they also note methodological omissions (no statistical significance testing, limited ablation/isolation of controls, and no released code) that weaken reproducibility and full technical confidence. Reviewer A further highlights the lack of theoretical motivation and some unsupported assumptions, while Reviewer B views these as modest issues. Given the credible empirical improvements but important methodological gaps, a middling score of 3 reflects sound experimental promise tempered by shortcomings in rigor and reproducibility.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear claims and presents experimental results on three datasets showing ~5–10% gains and evaluates several contextual LSTM variants, but it lacks theoretical justification for why the hierarchical fusion should work beyond empirical observation. The experimental methodology is incomplete from a theory-first perspective: there is no mention of statistical significance testing, limited ablation isolation (e.g., same-parameter flat-LSTM control not reported), and no code release to aid reproducibility despite reporting hyperparameters. Some unsupported assumptions remain (context always helpful; biasing by neighbors not systematically addressed) so while the results are promising they are not fully convincing technically.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments on three standard datasets (MOSI, MOUD, IEMOCAP) showing consistent 5–10% gains over unimodal baselines and prior multimodal methods, and hyperparameters/details are reported for reproducibility. There are no formal mathematical derivations to verify, and the proposed hierarchical/contextual LSTM variants are evaluated against reasonable baselines (uni-SVM, prior MKL methods). My concerns are modest: the paper does not report statistical significance testing or cross-validation robustness, and cross-dataset generalization issues (language mismatch) suggest limits to claimed generality. Overall the experimental evidence credibly supports the main technical claims but minor methodological omissions prevent a perfect score.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the empirical evaluation is solid—using three standard datasets, multiple contextual LSTM variants, and useful ablation-style comparisons—and that reporting of hyperparameters and failure modes is adequate. However, both note shortcomings in statistical significance/variance reporting, lack of released code, and limited sensitivity or cross-dataset generalization analyses; these issues prevent a top score but do not undermine the overall experimental contribution, so a score of 4 is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The experimental evaluation is adequate in that it uses three datasets (MOSI, MOUD, IEMOCAP) and compares multiple contextual LSTM variants and fusion schemes, but it lacks statistical significance testing and clear isolations that a theory-focused reader would expect (e.g., controlled same-parameter flat LSTM baselines or formal analysis explaining why hierarchical fusion should work). Ablations are present at a coarse level (sc-LSTM, bc-LSTM, h-LSTM and hierarchical vs concatenation) but key component-level sensitivity analyses and detailed failure-mode quantification are missing. Related work coverage and reporting of hyperparameters are acceptable, but reproducibility is weakened by no mention of released code and absence of rigorous significance/variance reporting.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments are solid: evaluation on three standard datasets (MOSI, MOUD, IEMOCAP) and reported 5–10% gains over prior multimodal baselines (Poria et al., Rozgic) support the main claims. The paper explores several contextual LSTM variants and contrasts hierarchical fusion with non-hierarchical fusion, which provides useful ablation-style comparisons, and hyperparameters and feature extraction details are reported to aid reproducibility. Related work coverage is adequate and the authors explicitly discuss failure modes (noisy audio, face detection issues, cross-dataset language mismatch). I withhold a top score because cross-dataset generalization is weak, code isn’t mentioned, and it’s unclear whether exhaustive sensitivity analyses (e.g., more ablations or statistical significance tests) were performed.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important practical problem and demonstrates consistent empirical gains (~5–10%) on standard benchmarks, making it likely to influence applied work. They also concur it is more of an empirical/engineering advance than a theoretical breakthrough and that reproducibility/ablation details are limited; despite these limitations, the consistent performance improvements justify a solid positive impact score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles a meaningful problem—modeling contextual utterance relationships for multimodal sentiment—which is practically important and shows consistent empirical gains (≈5–10%) on established datasets (MOSI, MOUD, IEMOCAP). However, from a theory-first perspective the contribution is primarily an empirical/architectural tweak (hierarchical contextual LSTMs and fusion) with no principled analysis explaining why the hierarchy should work, and the summary does not indicate released code or extensive ablation/statistical testing to isolate components. Thus the work is useful and likely to be adopted by practitioners in the area, but it does not appear transformative or likely to shift foundational thinking without stronger theoretical justification and reproducibility.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an important practical problem—contextual modeling for multimodal sentiment—which matters for many downstream applications. The hierarchical/contextual LSTM design yields reported consistent gains (≈5–10%) over prior multimodal methods on standard benchmarks (MOSI, MOUD, IEMOCAP), which is the kind of empirical improvement that will influence applied work. The approach is a clear and reproducible engineering advance that should be adopted and extended (e.g., attention-based context), so it opens useful follow-up directions, though it is not a theoretical breakthrough and lacks evidence of broad cross-dataset generalization or released code.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper is a good fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it addresses multimodal sentiment analysis, proposes a contextual/hierarchical LSTM approach, and shows consistent empirical gains on standard benchmarks. I award a 4 because, while the empirical results and positioning are strong, concerns about limited theoretical justification and lack of clear reproducibility (e.g., released code) temper a top score.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses a core multimodal sentiment-analysis problem using sequence models (contextual/hierarchical LSTMs) and evaluates on standard datasets (MOSI, MOUD, IEMOCAP), so it sits squarely within the scope and audience of top NLP/ML venues. My reservation is that the work appears mostly empirical with little theoretical justification of the hierarchical/contextual mechanism and no mention of released code for reproducibility; these issues are scope concerns but do not preclude relevance to the venue.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is a strong fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it addresses multimodal sentiment analysis (a core applied NLP/ML topic), proposes a well-motivated contextual/hierarchical LSTM fusion architecture, and reports consistent empirical gains (≈5–10%) over prior multimodal methods (Poria et al., 2015; Rozgic et al., 2012b) on standard benchmarks (MOSI, MOUD, IEMOCAP). The methodology and evaluation (unimodal feature extraction, contextual LSTMs, clear baselines, and hyperparameter reporting) meet the empirical standards I expect, and the paper is positioned appropriately within the multimodal sentiment/emotion community.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the work is an incremental, pragmatic engineering contribution that combines existing components (text-CNN, 3D-CNN, openSMILE, LSTMs) and applies hierarchical/contextual LSTMs for multimodal fusion. The paper delivers consistent empirical gains, which makes it a useful contribution, but it lacks a strong theoretical novelty or a fundamentally new modeling principle. Given the moderate novelty balanced by meaningful empirical improvement, a score of 3 is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a hierarchical/contextual application of LSTMs for multimodal fusion, which appears to be a straightforward combination of existing components (text-CNN, 3D-CNN, openSMILE, and LSTMs) rather than a genuinely new modeling principle. The problem (multimodal sentiment analysis) is well-studied and the proposed hierarchical fusion is a non-trivial engineering extension but lacks theoretical motivation or novel formalization; comparisons are to prior multimodal works but do not establish a fundamentally new perspective. Given the empirical improvements, the contribution is useful but largely incremental rather than surprising to an expert in the field.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper combines known components (text-CNN, 3D-CNN, openSMILE features, and LSTMs) but proposes a pragmatic hierarchical fusion that stacks a second-level contextual LSTM to model inter-utterance dependencies before multimodal fusion. The authors clearly contrast this approach with prior multimodal fusion work (Poria et al. 2015; Rozgic et al. 2012b) and report consistent 5–10% gains, so the technique is a useful, non-trivial engineering extension. However, the core ideas (contextual LSTMs and hierarchical modeling) are incremental rather than fundamentally new, so the contribution is moderate novelty rather than a major conceptual breakthrough.

main_claims: Propose a contextual LSTM architecture that models dependencies among utterances in a video for multimodal sentiment analysis.

Introduce a hierarchical fusion framework that first learns context-sensitive unimodal features via LSTMs and then fuses them with a second-level contextual LSTM, which improves performance over non-hierarchical fusion.

Demonstrate that the proposed context-aware methods outperform unimodal baselines (uni-SVM) and prior state-of-the-art multimodal methods (Poria et al., 2015; Rozgic et al., 2012b), with reported gains of about 5–10%.
_summary.methodology: Extract context-independent unimodal features (text via text-CNN with 300-d word2vec, video via 3D-CNN, audio via openSMILE), feed them into contextual LSTM variants (sc-LSTM, bc-LSTM, h-LSTM) to obtain context-sensitive features, and perform multimodal fusion either by concatenation (non-hierarchical) or by a two-level hierarchical LSTM fusion.
limitations: Failures occur when faces are hard to recognize or audio is noisy.
Model can be biased by surrounding utterances for weak/non-contextual sentiments leading to wrong predictions; authors suggest a context-aware attention mechanism as a fix.
Poor cross-dataset generalization for audio and textual modalities across languages (MOSI trained model tested on MOUD performed poorly due to language mismatch).
_summary.topic_area: Multimodal sentiment analysis / multimodal emotion recognition

reviewer_a.name: Theory-first Skeptic
reviewer_a.background: Senior researcher in machine learning with a focus on theoretical foundations of sequence models and representation learning; long experience reviewing for NeurIPS and ICLR.
traits: Penalises papers that present primarily empirical/engineering improvements without accompanying theoretical justification, formalization, or analysis (e.g., no proof, bound, or principled argument explaining why the architecture should work).

Requires thorough component-level ablation studies that isolate the contribution of each module; will deduct points if key ablations are missing or shallow (e.g., no tests of hierarchical LSTM vs same-parameter flat LSTM).

Demands statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) and evaluation on multiple diverse datasets; penalises claims based on single-dataset gains without significance reporting.
Penalises papers that lack clear reproducibility artifacts: full hyperparameter details, training regimes, or released code.
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Evaluates novelty and contribution primarily by theoretical clarity and isolation of mechanism; favors papers that explain why something works, not only that it works.

reviewer_b.name: Pragmatic Empiricist
reviewer_b.background: Principal investigator working on multimodal ML and applied NLP with many conference publications demonstrating applied architectures and thorough experimental evaluations; regular reviewer for ACL and EMNLP.
traits: Rewards papers that demonstrate consistent empirical gains (>3%) over strong baselines and prior state-of-the-art across tasks or meaningful evaluation splits.
Values clear, well-motivated architectural/engineering contributions (e.g., hierarchical/contextual modeling) that improve practical performance on real multimodal benchmarks.

Prefers papers with comprehensive empirical comparisons to prior SOTA and reasonable unimodal and multimodal baselines; will score positively if baselines are competitive and experiments are well-organized.
Positively weights papers that provide enough experimental detail (feature extraction, model configs) to be reproducible, even if no formal theory is provided.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Focuses on empirical rigor and practical impact: judges work by the strength and clarity of experiments and whether the method advances state-of-the-art in a meaningful, reproducible way.
